core_concept.principle: Chemical bonds form when atoms interact to achieve stable electron configurations, typically by gaining, losing, or sharing valence electrons.
core_concept.explanation: Valence electrons—the electrons in the outermost energy level—determine how atoms bond. Atoms bond to achieve stable electron configurations, often resembling noble gases. Understanding valence electrons is essential for predicting bond types, compound formulas, and chemical reactivity.

image.alt: Diagram showing valence electrons participating in bond formation between atoms
image.caption: Valence electrons are the outermost electrons involved in chemical bonding.

sections.introduction: Chemical bonds are the attractive forces that hold atoms together in compounds. These bonds form because bonded atoms are more stable (lower in energy) than isolated atoms.

introduction_image.alt: Energy diagram showing bonded atoms at lower energy than separate atoms
introduction_image.caption: Bond formation releases energy, making bonded atoms more stable.

key_concept.definition: A chemical bond is the attractive force that holds two or more atoms together in a compound, resulting from the interaction of valence electrons.
key_concept.context: Chemical bonds form because atoms seek stable electron configurations. The three main types are ionic bonds (electron transfer), covalent bonds (electron sharing), and metallic bonds (delocalized electrons).

image.alt: Diagram comparing ionic, covalent, and metallic bonding
image.caption: Different bond types result from different valence electron interactions.

sections.introduction: Valence electrons are the electrons in the outermost principal energy level of an atom. These are the only electrons involved in chemical bonding.

classifications.value: For main group elements, the group number indicates the number of valence electrons
characteristics: Transition metals: variable valence electrons from $s$ and $d$ orbitals
classifications.behavior: Valence electrons are farther from the nucleus and less tightly held than core electrons, making them available for bonding interactions.

examples.explanation: Sodium ($[Ne] 3s^1$) has one valence electron in the $3s$ orbital. This electron is easily lost to form $Na^+$, achieving a stable noble gas configuration.

image.alt: Sodium electron configuration highlighting valence electron
image.caption: Sodium's single valence electron is easily lost in bonding.

examples.explanation: Chlorine ($[Ne] 3s^2 3p^5$) has seven valence electrons. It readily gains one electron to form $Cl^-$, completing its octet.

image.alt: Chlorine electron configuration highlighting valence electrons
image.caption: Chlorine needs one more electron to complete its valence shell.

classifications.value: Lewis dot symbols represent valence electrons as dots around the element symbol
characteristics: Dots placed singly on four sides before pairing
Useful for predicting bond formation and compound formulas
classifications.behavior: Lewis symbols show at a glance how many electrons an atom can lose, gain, or share to achieve stability.

examples.explanation: Carbon (4 valence electrons): $\cdot \dot{C} \cdot$; Oxygen (6 valence electrons): $:\ddot{O}:$. These symbols predict carbon forms 4 bonds, oxygen forms 2 bonds.

image.alt: Lewis dot symbols for common elements
image.caption: Lewis symbols visualize valence electrons for bonding predictions.

sections.section_title: 5.1.3 The Drive for Stability: Noble Gas Configurations
sections.introduction: Atoms bond to achieve stable electron configurations, typically matching the electron configuration of the nearest noble gas.

principles.statement: Noble gases (Group 18) have full valence shells ($ns^2 np^6$, except He: $1s^2$), making them exceptionally stable and unreactive.
principles.application: Other atoms gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve noble gas configurations. This drive for stability is the fundamental reason chemical bonds form.
principles.analogy: Like completing a puzzle: atoms 'want' full valence shells just as a puzzle 'wants' all its pieces. Bonding helps atoms complete their electron 'puzzles.'
principles.example: Sodium ($[Ne] 3s^1$) loses one electron to achieve $[Ne]$ configuration; chlorine ($[Ne] 3s^2 3p^5$) gains one electron to achieve $[Ar]$ configuration.

image.alt: Electron configurations showing atoms achieving noble gas configurations
image.caption: Atoms bond to achieve stable noble gas electron configurations.

principles.statement: Bond formation releases energy because bonded atoms are at lower energy (more stable) than isolated atoms.
principles.application: The energy released when bonds form (bond energy) must be supplied to break those bonds. This energy balance determines reaction spontaneity and compound stability.
principles.analogy: Like a ball rolling downhill: atoms 'roll' to lower energy states by forming bonds, releasing energy in the process.
principles.example: When $H_2$ forms from two H atoms, $436$ kJ/mol is released. This same energy must be supplied to break the $H-H$ bond.

image.alt: Energy diagram showing energy release during bond formation
image.caption: Bond formation releases energy; bond breaking requires energy input.

sections.introduction: The way valence electrons interact determines the type of chemical bond that forms between atoms.

categories.description: Formed by complete transfer of electrons from metal to nonmetal, creating oppositely charged ions that attract electrostatically.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Metal atoms (low ionization energy) lose valence electrons to form cations; nonmetal atoms (high electron affinity) gain electrons to form anions. Electrostatic attraction between $+$ and $-$ ions forms the ionic bond.

examples.explanation: Na loses one electron: $Na \rightarrow Na^+ + e^-$; Cl gains one electron: $Cl + e^- \rightarrow Cl^-$. Electrostatic attraction forms $Na^+Cl^-$ ionic bond.

image.alt: Electron transfer from Na to Cl forming ions
image.caption: Ionic bonds form through electron transfer and electrostatic attraction.

categories.description: Formed by sharing of valence electrons between nonmetal atoms.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Atoms share electron pairs to achieve stable configurations. Shared electrons are attracted to both nuclei simultaneously, holding atoms together.

examples.explanation: Two H atoms ($1s^1$) share their electrons: $H \cdot + \cdot H \rightarrow H:H$ or $H-H$. Each H achieves $He$ configuration ($1s^2$) through sharing.

image.caption: Covalent bonds form through electron sharing between atoms.

categories.description: Formed by attraction between metal cations and a 'sea' of delocalized valence electrons.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Metal atoms release valence electrons into a shared 'sea' that moves freely throughout the metal lattice. Positive metal ions are held together by attraction to the delocalized electrons.

examples.explanation: Cu atoms ($[Ar] 4s^1 3d^{10}$) release $4s$ electrons into a delocalized sea. The $Cu^+$ ions are held in a lattice by attraction to the mobile electrons, enabling conductivity.

image.alt: Metal lattice with delocalized electron sea
image.caption: Metallic bonds involve delocalized electrons shared among many atoms.

sections.section_title: 5.1.4 Predicting Bond Type from Element Properties
sections.introduction: The types of elements involved and their electronegativity differences help predict which type of bond will form.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Bond Type from Element Pairs
worked_examples.problem: Predict the bond type for each pair: (a) Na and Cl, (b) C and O, (c) Fe and Fe. Explain your reasoning using valence electrons and element properties.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze element types: Na is metal (Group 1), Cl is nonmetal (Group 17). Metals tend to lose electrons; nonmetals tend to gain electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: Na (metal) + Cl (nonmetal) → electron transfer likely → ionic bond

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze element types: C and O are both nonmetals (Groups 14 and 16). Nonmetals tend to share electrons rather than transfer them.
step_by_step.calculation: C (nonmetal) + O (nonmetal) → electron sharing likely → covalent bond

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze element types: Fe is a transition metal. Metal-metal interactions involve delocalized electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: Fe (metal) + Fe (metal) → delocalized electrons → metallic bond

step_by_step.explanation: Verify predictions using electronegativity differences: Na-Cl $\Delta$EN = 2.1 (ionic); C-O $\Delta$EN = 1.0 (polar covalent); Fe-Fe $\Delta$EN = 0 (metallic).

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Na-Cl: ionic bond (metal + nonmetal, large $\Delta$EN); (b) C-O: covalent bond (nonmetal + nonmetal, moderate $\Delta$EN); (c) Fe-Fe: metallic bond (metal + metal).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Bond type is predicted by element types and electronegativity differences: metal + nonmetal → ionic; nonmetal + nonmetal → covalent; metal + metal → metallic.

image.alt: Flowchart for predicting bond type from element properties
image.caption: Element properties and electronegativity differences predict bond type.

sections.section_title: 5.1.5 Valence Electrons and the Periodic Table
sections.introduction: An element's position in the periodic table directly indicates its valence electron count and bonding behavior.

process_flow.description: For Groups 1, 2, and 13-18, the group number indicates valence electron count.
process_flow.mechanism: Group 1: $ns^1$ (1 valence $e^-$); Group 2: $ns^2$ (2 valence $e^-$); Group 13: $ns^2 np^1$ (3 valence $e^-$); ... Group 18: $ns^2 np^6$ (8 valence $e^-$, except He).
process_flow.result: Valence electron count predicts ion charge and bonding capacity.

image.alt: Periodic table showing valence electrons by group
image.caption: Group number predicts valence electron count for main group elements.

process_flow.description: Transition metals (Groups 3-12) have variable valence electrons from both $s$ and $d$ orbitals.
process_flow.mechanism: Configuration $(n-1)d^{1-10} ns^{0-2}$ allows multiple oxidation states. Valence electrons can come from $ns$ and/or $(n-1)d$ orbitals.
process_flow.result: Transition metals exhibit variable bonding and multiple oxidation states.

image.caption: Transition metals have variable valence electrons from $s$ and $d$ orbitals.

process_flow.description: Valence electron count determines typical bonding behavior and ion formation.
process_flow.mechanism: Atoms with 1-3 valence electrons tend to lose electrons (form cations); atoms with 5-7 valence electrons tend to gain electrons (form anions); atoms with 4 valence electrons tend to share (covalent).
process_flow.result: Periodic position predicts whether an element forms ionic or covalent bonds.

image.alt: Periodic table showing bonding tendencies by region
image.caption: Valence electron count predicts bonding behavior across the periodic table.

sections.introduction: Understanding why valence electrons participate in bonding requires considering energy, distance from nucleus, and electron-electron interactions.

principles.statement: Valence electrons are in the highest occupied energy level, farthest from the nucleus, and least tightly held.
principles.application: Because valence electrons experience weaker nuclear attraction than core electrons, they require less energy to be involved in bonding interactions (transfer or sharing).
principles.analogy: Like fruit on a tree: valence electrons are like fruit on the outer branches—easier to pick (involve in bonding) than fruit near the trunk (core electrons).
principles.example: Sodium's $3s^1$ valence electron requires only $496$ kJ/mol to remove (first IE), while removing a core $2p$ electron requires $>4500$ kJ/mol.

image.alt: Energy level diagram showing valence vs. core electron energies
image.caption: Valence electrons are higher in energy and less tightly held than core electrons.

principles.statement: Valence electrons experience repulsion from other valence electrons, making them more available for bonding.
principles.application: In atoms with multiple valence electrons, electron-electron repulsion reduces the effective nuclear attraction, making these electrons more available for sharing or transfer.
principles.analogy: Like people in a crowded room: valence electrons 'push' against each other, making it easier for one to 'leave' (participate in bonding).
principles.example: Oxygen ($2s^2 2p^4$) has electron-electron repulsion in its $2p$ subshell, making it easier to gain two electrons (forming $O^{2-}$) than to remove electrons.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron-electron repulsion in valence shell
image.caption: Electron-electron repulsion in valence shells facilitates bonding.

summary: Chemical bonds form when atoms interact to achieve stable electron configurations through their valence electrons—the electrons in the outermost energy level. Valence electrons determine bonding behavior: Group number indicates valence electron count for main group elements ($ns^1$ for Group 1, $ns^2 np^5$ for Group 17, etc.). Atoms bond to achieve noble gas configurations ($ns^2 np^6$), which are exceptionally stable. Three main bond types exist: ionic bonds form by electron transfer from metal to nonmetal (e.g., $Na \rightarrow Na^+ + e^-$, $Cl + e^- \rightarrow Cl^-$, then $Na^+Cl^-$); covalent bonds form by electron sharing between nonmetals (e.g., $H \cdot + \cdot H \rightarrow H-H$); metallic bonds form by attraction between metal cations and delocalized valence electrons. Bond formation releases energy because bonded atoms are more stable than isolated atoms. Valence electrons participate in bonding because they are farthest from the nucleus, least tightly held, and experience electron-electron repulsion. Element position in the periodic table predicts bonding behavior: metals (left) tend to lose electrons; nonmetals (right) tend to gain or share electrons; electronegativity differences help predict bond type. Lewis dot symbols visualize valence electrons for bonding predictions. Understanding valence electrons and bonding fundamentals enables prediction of compound formation, formulas, and properties.
